original_hypothesis.title: Reentrancy in executeFeeSwap via Malicious userToken Drains validatorToken Reserves
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: executeFeeSwap performs IERC20(userToken).transferFrom before updating pool.reserveUserToken and pool.reserveValidatorToken. If userToken is a malicious/ERC-777 token controlled by the attacker, the callback during transferFrom allows recursive reentry into executeFeeSwap. Since reserveValidatorToken has not been decremented by any prior call in the reentrancy chain, each reentrant invocation passes the sufficiency check and sends out amountOut of real validatorTokens. The attacker drains the entire validatorToken reserve while the malicious token sends nothing (or minimal tokens) on reentrant calls.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code clearly shows external call before state update on lines ~119-124. No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant) is present anywhere in the contract. The attacker controls the userToken contract and can create pools permissionlessly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom at line ~119 executes BEFORE pool.reserveUserToken += and pool.reserveValidatorToken -= at lines ~121-122, and BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transfer at line ~124

preconditions: Attacker deploys a malicious ERC-777/callback-enabled token as userToken
Pool exists with real validatorToken (e.g., USDC) and the malicious userToken, with liquidity from other LPs
No reentrancy guard on executeFeeSwap (verified: none exists)
validatorToken is a standard ERC-20 that successfully transfers

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external call (transferFrom) before state update (reserve increment/decrement)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: pool.reserveValidatorToken not decremented before userToken.transferFrom callback; attacker can recursively drain validatorToken
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No ReentrancyGuard / nonReentrant modifier; no checks-effects-interactions pattern

analysis_questions: Can a malicious userToken's transferFrom callback successfully reenter executeFeeSwap multiple times?
How many recursive calls are possible before gas runs out (stack depth limit ~1024)?
Does the uint128 arithmetic revert on the final state update when nested calls unwind?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious ERC-777-like token with transferFrom callback
Create pool and seed with real validatorToken
Execute reentrancy attack and measure extracted validatorTokens vs deposited

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys malicious userToken with callback hook. Creates pool (maliciousToken/USDC). LPs deposit USDC. Attacker calls executeFeeSwap; during transferFrom, malicious token reenters executeFeeSwap N times. Each call extracts amountOut USDC from unchanged reserves. Total drain: N * amountOut, limited only by pool's USDC reserve and gas.
prerequisites: Pool with real validatorToken liquidity from other LPs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of all validatorToken reserves in affected pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LPs who deposited validatorToken to pools using malicious userToken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - funds irrecoverably transferred to attacker

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for executeFeeSwap with callback-enabled tokens

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~30K gas per recursive call; estimate max recursion depth
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal - attacker deploys worthless token, needs gas only
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via internal transaction traces showing recursive executeFeeSwap calls

validation_result.mechanism: reentrancy via malicious userToken callback before state update
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious userToken can reenter executeFeeSwap during transferFrom, bypassing the reserveValidatorToken sufficiency check on each reentrant call and draining the entire validatorToken reserve from the pool.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The executeFeeSwap function performs an external call to IERC20(userToken).transferFrom before decrementing pool.reserveValidatorToken. Since pools are permissionless and userToken is attacker-controlled, the attacker deploys a malicious ERC-20 whose transferFrom triggers a callback that reenters executeFeeSwap. On each reentrant call, pool.reserveValidatorToken remains at its original value R (no state has been updated yet in any outer frame), so the sufficiency check `pool.reserveValidatorToken < amountOut` passes every time. Each call, upon unwinding, executes `IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(msg.sender, amountOut)`, sending real validatorTokens. The state decrements accumulate during unwinding: after N calls, reserveValidatorToken = R - N*amountOut. As long as N*amountOut <= R, no underflow occurs and the entire transaction succeeds. The malicious token's transferFrom returns true without transferring real value, so the attacker pays nothing but receives N*amountOut of real validatorTokens. There is no reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) anywhere in the contract, and no SafeERC20 is used to check return values.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code at FeeAMM.sol lines ~109-130 clearly shows: (1) reserve sufficiency check at line ~117, (2) external call IERC20(userToken).transferFrom at line ~119, (3) state updates pool.reserveUserToken += and pool.reserveValidatorToken -= at lines ~121-122, (4) second external call IERC20(validatorToken).transfer at line ~124. The state updates occur AFTER the first external call, violating checks-effects-interactions. No ReentrancyGuard or nonReentrant modifier is present in the contract. The contract imports only IERC20 from OpenZeppelin, not SafeERC20 or ReentrancyGuard.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom is an external call to an attacker-controlled contract (the malicious userToken). This call occurs BEFORE pool.reserveValidatorToken is decremented. During the callback in transferFrom, the attacker reenters executeFeeSwap. Since reserveValidatorToken has not been decremented by any prior call in the reentrancy chain, the sufficiency check passes on every reentrant invocation. Each invocation, upon unwinding, transfers real validatorTokens via IERC20(validatorToken).transfer. The malicious token can return true without actually sending tokens, so the attacker pays nothing.

calling_sequence: 2. poolId computed, pool storage pointer obtained
9. Steps 2-5 repeat: reserveValidatorToken is STILL R (unchanged), check passes again
10. Steps 6-9 repeat N times until attacker decides to stop reentering

11. Deepest call: transferFrom returns true (no real transfer), state updates: reserveUserToken += amountIn, reserveValidatorToken -= amountOut (now R - amountOut), IERC20(USDC).transfer sends real USDC
12. Each outer frame unwinds: state updates accumulate (reserveValidatorToken goes R-amountOut, R-2*amountOut, ..., R-N*amountOut), each frame sends amountOut real USDC
13. Total USDC extracted: N * amountOut, total malicious tokens 'deposited': 0 (or fake amounts)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is at FeeAMM.sol line ~119 where IERC20(userToken).transferFrom is executed. At this point, pool.reserveValidatorToken still equals its pre-call value R. The state update at lines ~121-122 (pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn); pool.reserveValidatorToken -= uint128(amountOut)) has not executed. The attacker's malicious userToken callback reenters executeFeeSwap, which reads the same stale reserveValidatorToken = R at line ~117, passes the check, and proceeds to extract more validatorTokens. This pattern repeats until the attacker chooses to stop. During unwinding, each frame decrements reserveValidatorToken, and as long as N * amountOut <= R, no underflow occurs (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic). Each frame also calls IERC20(validatorToken).transfer at line ~124, sending real validatorTokens to the attacker.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The foundry.toml confirms Solidity 0.8.28 compiler and OpenZeppelin dependency, consistent with the source code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is fully valid: (1) Attacker deploys MaliciousToken with a transferFrom that triggers a callback and returns true without actually transferring tokens. (2) A pool is created with MaliciousToken as userToken and a real token (e.g., USDC) as validatorToken. (3) Liquidity is provided by LPs depositing USDC via mint(). (4) Attacker's contract calls executeFeeSwap. During the malicious transferFrom callback, it reenters executeFeeSwap N times. (5) Each call passes the reserve check against the unchanged reserveValidatorToken. (6) During unwinding, each call transfers real USDC and decrements reserves. (7) Total extraction: N * amountOut USDC for zero cost. Gas limits the recursion depth (~1024 stack frames, or ~30-50K gas per frame allowing hundreds of reentrant calls), but the attacker can also batch multiple transactions.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Complete drainage of all validatorToken (e.g., USDC) reserves in any pool where the userToken is attacker-controlled. Any liquidity provider who deposited validatorToken into such a pool loses their entire deposit. The attack requires zero capital (the malicious token transfers nothing) and a single atomic transaction. Recovery is impossible as funds are irrecoverably transferred to the attacker.
recommended_mitigations: Add OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard and apply nonReentrant modifier to executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint, and burn
Reorder executeFeeSwap to follow checks-effects-interactions: update pool.reserveUserToken and pool.reserveValidatorToken BEFORE calling IERC20(userToken).transferFrom
Use OpenZeppelin SafeERC20 (safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom) for all token operations to handle non-standard return values
Consider adding a token whitelist or validation mechanism to prevent pools with malicious tokens
Apply the same checks-effects-interactions fix to burn() which has the same vulnerability pattern (transfers before state updates)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a MaliciousToken contract with transferFrom that (a) returns true, (b) does not actually transfer tokens, and (c) on the first N calls, triggers a callback to the AttackerContract. 2. Deploy FeeAMM. 3. Deploy AttackerContract that: in its callback, calls FeeAMM.executeFeeSwap(MaliciousToken, USDC, amountIn) up to N times. 4. Create pool via mint(MaliciousToken, USDC, initialUSDC, someLP) from a separate LP account. 5. From AttackerContract, call executeFeeSwap. 6. Assert: AttackerContract's USDC balance increased by N * amountOut. Assert: pool.reserveValidatorToken decreased by N * amountOut. Assert: no real MaliciousTokens were transferred to FeeAMM.
uncertainties: Maximum recursion depth depends on gas limit and per-call gas cost; EVM stack depth limit is 1024 but gas will likely be exhausted before that
Some ERC-20 implementations may have internal guards that affect the callback mechanism, but since the attacker fully controls the malicious token this is not a real limitation
additional_testing_needed: Foundry test with a custom MaliciousToken implementing reentrancy callback in transferFrom
Gas profiling to determine maximum number of reentrant calls per transaction at various gas limits
Verify that the same reentrancy vector exists in burn() (transfers before state updates at lines ~236-243)
Test cross-function reentrancy: e.g., from executeFeeSwap callback into burn or rebalanceSwap
